era_context: The Hellenistic world in its final century before Roman dominance. The Seleucid Empire is fragmenting under weak successors; Egypt under the Ptolemies remains a major power but internally unstable; the Roman Republic is consolidating control over the Mediterranean following victories in Macedonia and Syria. In China, the Han Dynasty is in its middle period under strong imperial rule. This is an era of dynastic decline in the Near East and the rise of Rome as a hegemonic force.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier knowledge
Central Asia (sparse Greek and Chinese records)
Mesoamerica (no contemporary written records in Old World sources)

events.title: Death of Demetrius II Nicator of the Seleucid Empire
events.description: Demetrius II Nicator, Seleucid king, died in 145 BCE after a tumultuous reign marked by military defeats and internal instability. His death precipitated a succession crisis that further weakened Seleucid authority. The empire was already losing control of eastern territories to the Parthian Empire and facing challenges from Egyptian interference and internal dynastic conflict. His death symbolizes the accelerating decline of Seleucid power in the mid-2nd century BCE.
events.certainty_note: Dating of Demetrius II's death is established through Seleucid chronology reconstructed from multiple later sources and numismatic evidence. Exact year has minor scholarly variation (145–143 BCE range), but 145 is conventional.

events.title: Accession of Antiochus VII Sidetes to Seleucid throne
events.description: Following Demetrius II's death, Antiochus VII Sidetes (also called Euergetes) ascended to the Seleucid throne. He was the last strong Seleucid king and attempted to restore imperial authority through military campaigns against the Parthians and the Hasmonean Jewish kingdom. His reign (145–129 BCE) represented a brief resurgence of Seleucid power before the empire's final fragmentation. He campaigned in Parthia and Judaea, temporarily reasserting control but ultimately unable to reverse the empire's decline.
events.certainty_note: Accession date of Antiochus VII is well-established through multiple sources including contemporary Jewish texts and later Greek historiography. Numismatic evidence also confirms his reign.

events.title: Hasmonean Judaea under John Hyrcanus I
events.description: John Hyrcanus I (ruled c. 135–104 BCE) was consolidating Hasmonean control over Judaea in 145 BCE, though he had not yet fully assumed power (his mother Salome Alexandra was regent until his majority). The Hasmonean dynasty, established through the Maccabean Revolt against Seleucid rule, was expanding its territory and establishing itself as a major regional power. This period saw the development of Jewish religious and political institutions that would define Judaea for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The exact chronology of Hasmonean succession is debated among scholars. Dating of John Hyrcanus I's reign varies (135–104 or 134–104 BCE). The year 145 BCE falls within the accepted range of his rule or regency period.
cross_references: Part of broader Hasmonean expansion narrative

events.title: Han Dynasty consolidation under Emperor Jing
events.description: Emperor Jing of Han (r. 157–141 BCE) was in the middle of his reign in 145 BCE, overseeing a period of relative stability and economic prosperity in the Han Dynasty. The empire had recovered from the Rebellion of the Seven States (154–151 BCE) and was consolidating centralized authority. This period saw the development of Confucian bureaucratic institutions and the expansion of Han influence. The reign of Emperor Jing is characterized by careful fiscal management and the strengthening of imperial institutions.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: Han Dynasty chronology is well-established through Chinese historical records and archaeological evidence. Emperor Jing's reign dates are securely fixed.

events.title: Parthian Empire expansion under Mithridates I
events.description: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE) was in the later stages of his reign, having transformed the Parthian Empire from a minor kingdom into a major regional power. By 145 BCE, he had conquered Mesopotamia from the Seleucids and was consolidating control over the Iranian plateau. His military campaigns and administrative reforms established Parthia as the dominant power in the Near East, a position it would maintain for centuries. This expansion directly contributed to Seleucid decline.
key_figures: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE)
events.certainty_note: Parthian chronology is less precisely dated than Greek or Chinese sources. Mithridates I's reign is estimated c. 171–138 BCE based on numismatic and later textual evidence. The exact year 145 BCE is approximate within this broader context.
cross_references: Part of broader shift in Near Eastern power dynamics

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 145 BCE as a turning point in Seleucid history is somewhat overstated in traditional narratives. While Demetrius II's death did precipitate succession instability, Antiochus VII's accession and subsequent military campaigns (particularly against Parthia and Judaea) temporarily reversed Seleucid decline. Modern scholarship emphasizes that the Seleucid collapse was a gradual process spanning decades, not a sudden crisis in 145 BCE. Additionally, the dating of Demetrius II's death is debated among scholars, with some sources placing it in 143 BCE rather than 145 BCE. The 'decline' narrative itself reflects Hellenistic-era Greek historiography that may have exaggerated Seleucid weakness to justify Roman intervention.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 145 BCE is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean world and China, reflecting the survival of Greek, Roman, and Chinese written sources. The Seleucid succession is known primarily through later Greek and Jewish texts, not contemporary records. Parthian history relies heavily on numismatic evidence and later Greek accounts, which may contain biases. Chinese sources are abundant and contemporary. The record is sparse for sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and the Americas. The surviving sources reflect the perspectives of literate elites in major empires and may underrepresent peripheral regions, non-literate societies, and the experiences of common people.

graph_edges.note: Death of Demetrius II directly precipitated the accession of Antiochus VII
Antiochus VII's reign ended with his death in 129 BCE, marking the final collapse of Seleucid power
Parthian expansion under Mithridates I was concurrent with Seleucid decline under Demetrius II
Han Dynasty stability contrasts with Seleucid instability in the same period
